text: A moderate-duration dynamic stretching warm-up (10 minutes) significantly improves running economy at 75% of the second ventilatory threshold compared to no stretching or static stretching in recreational distance runners.
Dynamic stretching during warm-up significantly improves time to exhaustion and total running distance at vVO2max intensity compared to both no stretching and static stretching.
Moderate-duration static stretching (60 seconds per leg) during warm-up does not significantly improve running economy or running performance compared to no stretching.

The performance benefits of dynamic stretching in warm-up occur without any change in rating of perceived exertion, suggesting improved physiological efficiency rather than increased effort tolerance.
An optimal warm-up protocol for distance runners consists of 5 minutes of easy running followed by 10 minutes of dynamic stretching, rather than 15 minutes of continuous running alone.
The acute effects of stretching on running economy depend on the stretching modality: dynamic stretching improves economy while static stretching of the same duration does not.
